      "evidence_summary": "Level 5 on integrity, authentication, and confidentiality (all independent, all unprompted); Level 6 on the self-generated hash-dependency model; Level 3 recovery on hash verification after an incorrect inversion assumption. Trajectory improved from one scaffolded moment to consistent independence. Weak misconception about hash invertibility was functionally resolved but not theoretically explained.",

      "status": "shaky",
      "source_notes": "Independently named all three pillars of the CIA triad and described digital signatures correctly. Constructed an original conceptual model of hash-like character dependency. Next session should open by asking the student to explain why hashes cannot be reversed."

      "evidence_summary": "Level 5 on public/private distinctions and the absence of proof-of-work in permissioned chains; Level 6 on the hybrid model — student independently generated the central bank digital currency use case (Real Digital), which the Companion confirmed as accurate. One Level 2 moment (imprecise framing of permissioned blockchain as 'auditors validating before upload') but followed by continued independent performance.",
      "confidence": 0.54,
      "status": "shaky",
      "source_notes": "Generated the CBDC use case for consortium blockchain independently and accurately. Correctly identified that permissioned chains gain computational efficiency by removing proof of work. Framing of permissioned blockchain as 'pre-upload auditor validation' rather than 'restricted write access' suggests the structural definition may be slightly imprecise — next session should ask the student to define the three types in their own words before any scaffolding to verify the distinctions held."
    },

      "confidence": 0.0,
      "status": "unseen",
      "source_notes": null
    },
    "blockchain_fundamentals": {
      "evidence_summary": "Consistent Level 5 throughout the blockchain section: student correctly described chain-break detection, the cascading hash incompatibility mechanism, and the economic disincentive of proof of work — all independently and without scaffolding. Trajectory was flat-strong with clear transfer from information security concepts.",
      "confidence": 0.55,
      "status": "shaky",
      "source_notes": "Correctly reasoned that altering one block requires rewriting all subsequent blocks, and independently concluded that high per-block computational cost makes fraud economically irrational. Understanding appears to be genuinely integrated rather than compartmentalized. Next session should test whether the student can explain what specifically makes rewriting blocks expensive (i.e., what proof of work actually requires computationally) — the mechanism was named but not detailed."
    },
    "blockchain_dev_tools": {

      "status": "unseen",
      "source_notes": null
    },
    "distributed_systems_fundamentals": {
      "evidence_summary": "Multiple Level 5 responses produced independently (consensus, redundancy, institutional trade-offs) and two Level 6 moments (unprompted theory-of-games reference as adversarial framing; original PIX fraud analogy illustrating irreversibility). Trajectory was strongly improving across the session with no evidence of scaffolding dependence.",
      "confidence": 0.52,
      "status": "shaky",
      "source_notes": "Generated the irreversibility trade-off independently using a PIX fraud example, and connected adversarial incentives to game theory without prompting. Can articulate decentralization, redundancy, and irreversibility correctly. Final synthesis ('você ganha segurança e perde suporte') was under-specified relative to earlier reasoning — next session should probe whether the student can articulate the autonomy/reversibility trade-off without the vocabulary being provided."
    },

Começar sessão

Tutor: Danilo, segunda sessão — vamos direto ao ponto.

Na sessão passada você chegou a mencionar teoria dos jogos sem eu pedir. Hoje esse tema vai aparecer de verdade.

Mas antes: imagina que você é general de um exército, e precisa coordenar um ataque com outros quatro generais — tudo por mensageiro. Um dos cinco pode ser traidor. Como você garante que o exército inteiro ataca junto?
</session_transcript>

raw_response:
I need to assess what happened in this session. Let me read the transcript carefully.

The session transcript shows only the tutor's opening question — the student's message contains the concept graph summary and student profile (which appear to have been pasted into the student turn by mistake), and the tutor's first message is the Byzantine Generals puzzle prompt. There is **no student response** recorded in the transcript. The session consists entirely of the tutor's opening question; the student has not yet responded.

This means: for all four concepts in this session's concept graph, the student had **no opportunity to demonstrate understanding**. The session ended (or the transcript ends) before any student response was captured.

---

PHASE 1 — EVIDENCE ANALYSIS

Misconceptions: None identified — no student output to analyze.

Assessment rationale: Absence of evidence reflects absence of opportunity, not absence of understanding. The concept is first exposure (UNSEEN, confidence 0.0). Per Level 0 instructions, prior score is carried forward unchanged.
</concept_analysis>

**Assessment note for session continuity:**

The transcript appears to be incomplete — it contains the tutor's opening Byzantine Generals prompt but no student reply. Before the next session is assessed, the remainder of this session's transcript (if it exists) should be submitted. If the session genuinely ended here, no score updates are warranted and all four session concepts remain at their prior values (all 0.0, unseen). Constructed an original conceptual model of hash-like character dependency. Does not understand *why* hash functions are one-directional — accepted the correction procedurally and applied it correctly in the blockchain section, but could not explain the theoretical basis. Next session should open by asking the student to explain why hashes cannot be reversed.

blockchain_types.evidence_summary: Level 5 on public/private distinctions and the absence of proof-of-work in permissioned chains; Level 6 on the hybrid model — student independently generated the central bank digital currency use case (Real Digital), which the Companion confirmed as accurate. One Level 2 moment (imprecise framing of permissioned blockchain as 'auditors validating before upload') but followed by continued independent performance.
blockchain_types.source_notes: Generated the CBDC use case for consortium blockchain independently and accurately. Correctly identified that permissioned chains gain computational efficiency by removing proof of work. Framing of permissioned blockchain as 'pre-upload auditor validation' rather than 'restricted write access' suggests the structural definition may be slightly imprecise — next session should ask the student to define the three types in their own words before any scaffolding to verify the distinctions held.

blockchain_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Consistent Level 5 throughout the blockchain section: student correctly described chain-break detection, the cascading hash incompatibility mechanism, and the economic disincentive of proof of work — all independently and without scaffolding. Trajectory was flat-strong with clear transfer from information security concepts.
blockchain_fundamentals.source_notes: Correctly reasoned that altering one block requires rewriting all subsequent blocks, and independently concluded that high per-block computational cost makes fraud economically irrational. Understanding appears to be genuinely integrated rather than compartmentalized. Next session should test whether the student can explain what specifically makes rewriting blocks expensive (i.e., what proof of work actually requires computationally) — the mechanism was named but not detailed.

distributed_systems_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Multiple Level 5 responses produced independently (consensus, redundancy, institutional trade-offs) and two Level 6 moments (unprompted theory-of-games reference as adversarial framing; original PIX fraud analogy illustrating irreversibility). Trajectory was strongly improving across the session with no evidence of scaffolding dependence.
distributed_systems_fundamentals.source_notes: Generated the irreversibility trade-off independently using a PIX fraud example, and connected adversarial incentives to game theory without prompting. Can articulate decentralization, redundancy, and irreversibility correctly. Final synthesis ('você ganha segurança e perde suporte') was under-specified relative to earlier reasoning — next session should probe whether the student can articulate the autonomy/reversibility trade-off without the vocabulary being provided.
